It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. Jessica F. Green* Department of Environmental Studies, New York University, New York, NY, USA Abstract Non-state actors – including ﬁrms, non-governmental organizations, and networks – are now a permanent ﬁxture in environmental politics. However, we know surprisingly little about when states choose to delegate to non-state actors through multilateral treaties. This paper provides an historical picture, tracing patterns of delegation to non-state agents in a random sample of multilateral en- vironmental agreements from 1902 to 2002. I introduce a new unit of analysis – the policy function – to understand what non- state actors actually do as agents. I ﬁnd that analyses of delegation are sensitive to the unit of analysis; patterns of delegation at the treaty level are very different from those at the level of individual policy functions. While overall the decision to delegate to non- state actors – what I term transnational delegation – is rare, it has grown over time. Complex treaties, those with secretariats, and those focused on the management of nature are more apt to delegate to non-state actors. Non-state agents ﬁll a small, but growing role in multilateral environmental treaties. Keywords: delegation, environmental governance, private authority. 1. Introduction Non-state actors are now a permanent ﬁxture in environmental politics (Green 2014). Environmental non-governmental organizations (NGOs) lobby domestic governments in order to inﬂuence intergovernmental negotiations. Firms also seek inﬂuence through umbrella organizations, such as the World Business Council on Sustainable Development, as they present a united position on a variety of environmental matters. Outside of multilateral conferences, transnational advocates engage in contentious politics, seeking major revisions to the status quo. And increasingly, non-state actors of all stripes are creating their own rules and standards to address environmen- tal problems. One can now buy sustainably harvested timber, genetically modiﬁed (GMO)-free food, and even go on a certiﬁed-green vacation. Non-state actors have permeated global environmental politics. However, the instances in which states delegate to non-state actors through multilateral environmental treaties have received less examination. There is a considerable body of work on when and why states delegate to international organizations (IOs), but relatively little is known about when, much less why, states delegate to non-state actors. Delegation should not be overlooked as an important mode through which non-state actors exercise authority in global environmental politics. This paper begins to ﬁll this gap by providing an historical picture of delegation to non-state actors, what I term transnational delegation. I present data that traces patterns of delegation in a random sample of multilateral environ- mental agreements from 1902 to 2002. I disaggregate each treaty into its constituent functions, which allows us to see not only when states choose non-state actors as agents, but for what purposes. Using the policy function as the unit of analysis provides a detailed look at who is actually carrying out the “nuts and bolts” of governing. More importantly, by changing the unit of analysis from the treaty to the policy function, we see a very different picture of how non-state actors participate in international environmental governance. For example, the data show that while states delegate to non-state actors in approximately 20 percent of the treaties studied, when the unit of analysis shifts to the policy function, their role is much smaller – contributing to less than 4 percent of all policy functions. A comparison of our analyses shows that conclusions are very sensitive to the unit of analysis. Her analysis examines patterns of delegation at the treaty level, which demonstrates that it is a widespread phenomenon. However, when one disaggregates treaties to examine distinct policy functions, as I do, delegation occurs much less frequently. Finally, I employ descriptive inference to make some evidentiary-based generalizations about the practice of delegation to non-state actors. I eschew a grand theoretical explanation; rather, I focus on the diverse factors that are associated with delegation. These factors provide insights into transnational delegation – a set of practices about which we know very little. There is, I argue, great value in careful empirical work, even if it is not guided by formal empirical tests. As such, I do not seek to test these explanations against each other. Rather, the data and analysis presented here aim to inform “mid-level theory,” which is, admittedly, “less exciting than the Great Debates” that pits the “-isms” “against one another” (Lake 2013, p. 568). However, mid-level theory has its merits: its “analytic eclecticism” aims to form the basis for new, richer research agendas that push past the Great Debates (Sil & Katzenstein 2010). This paper heeds the call for eclecticism, by “recognizing and delineating relationships between concepts, observations and causal stories” (Sil & Katzenstein 2010, p. 2). Indeed, my hope is that careful descriptive inference can lay the foundation for future efforts to identify generalizable conditions. The paper proceeds as follows. The ﬁrst section deﬁnes key terms and provides examples of transnational to non-state actors. The second section reviews the relevant literature on delegation, focusing on non-state actors as agents. There is, of course, a vast literature, particularly in economics and American politics, about delegation, but these are not examined here. Rather, I survey the diverse literature that explores what we know about when states, acting collectively, choose to delegate to non-state actors. Section three describes a new dataset on delegation to non-state actors in multilateral environmental agreements, explaining how the data was gathered and coded. Section four provides basic descriptive statistics. Finally, section ﬁve presents the ﬁndings from the data. 2. Delegation at the transnational level Delegation can be understood as a “conditional grant of authority from a principal to an agent that empowers the latter to act on behalf of the former” (Hawkins et al. 2006, p. 7). In the context of international politics, delegation is deﬁned as “a grant of authority by two or more states to an international body to make decisions or take actions”(Bradley & Kelley 2008, p. 3; emphasis in original). In international relations, studies of delegation tend to focus on the international level, where the principal is a collective one. Thus, examples of delegation that conform to this deﬁnition include when states allow trade disputes to be adjudicated by the Dispute Settlement Body of the World Trade Organization, or the author- ity granted to the World Bank to fund and oversee development projects. These are both examples of states acting col- lectively: jointly, they oversee these IOs, which are then delegated authority to carry out a speciﬁc set of tasks. This study focuses on transnational delegation: delegation of authority by states, acting collectively, to non-state actors. This study deﬁnes non-state actors broadly, to include NGOs, ﬁrms, and transnational networks, as well as scientiﬁc and other expert bodies. (This list is meant to be indicative, rather than all-inclusive.) Thus, I use the term to include all actors that are neither states nor IOs. To be clear, this deﬁnition does not include instances in which individual states delegate to non-state actors. Thus, if the United States chose to outsource some of its military activities to private military contractors, this would not be included in the study, because the principal is not a collective but rather an individual state. A few examples from the data provide a better picture of what delegation to non-state actors looks like. The 1972 Convention for the Conservation of Antarctic Seals is one of ﬁve agreements that comprise the Antarctic Treaty System. It delegates monitoring functions to an NGO called the Scientiﬁc Committee on Antarctic Research (SCAR). SCAR is an interdisciplinary committee of another NGO, the International Council for Science, which provides scientiﬁc advice to multilateral agreements, including the 1972 Seal Convention. The treaty notes, “Each Contracting Party shall also provide to the other Contracting Parties and to SCAR…information on any steps it has taken in accordance with Article 2 of this Convention” (Mitchell 2002–2016). Thus, monitoring is simultaneously not delegated (as states are responsible) and delegated to a non-state actor. Another example is drawn from the 1958 Convention on Fishing And Conservation Of The Living Resources Of The High Seas (Mitchell 2002–2016). It states that “any dispute which may arise between States…shall, at the request J. F. Green Delegation in environmental governance 2 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 3: of any of the parties, be submitted for settlement to a special commission of ﬁve members.” Thus, states choose to delegate dispute settlement to an unspeciﬁed third party. Some treaties delegate to non-state actors for speciﬁc tasks. The 1983 agreement establishing the International Tropical Timber Organization states that the governing body “shall appoint independent auditors for the purpose of auditing the accounts of the Organization” (Mitchell 2002–2016). The 1946 Whaling Convention states that Parties may cooperate with (unspeciﬁed) private agencies to study whaling in order to collect and analyze statistical informa- tion on stocks. These examples provide a ﬂavor of what transnational delegation actually looks like in practice. Many different types of actors may be named – ranging from NGOs to independent bodies. And they may be tasked with a number of different responsibilities. 3. What do we know? While there is a sizable body of work on why states delegate to IOs, we know relatively little about transnational dele- gation. A few works consider non-state actors as a postscript to larger studies on international delegation. For example, Hawkins et al.’s (2006) edited volume examines delegation to IOs. Only one chapter – the last one – includes any con- sideration of non-state actors. Lake and McCubbins (2006) trace the potentially important role of NGOs as informa- tion providers through a series of theoretical models, rather than empirical analysis. Similarly, Bradley and Kelley’s (2008) work on international delegation includes only a small role for non-state actors. In their framework of delega- tion, they include a category called “redelegation,” in which agents may in turn enlist other agents to help carry out particular tasks, thus lengthening the chain of delegation. They note, “After states delegate to international bodies, these bodies often have the power to redelegate that authority to other international bodies or to other actors such as nongovernmental organizations” (Bradley & Kelley 2008, p. 5). Thus, non-state actors are listed as one of a number of possible agents, but little theorizing or data informs their discussion. A handful of works focus speciﬁcally on transnational delegation, although they do not label it speciﬁcally as such. Perhaps best known is Büthe and Mattli’s (2011) work on private regulation in global ﬁnancial and product markets. They identify the delegation of regulatory authority to private rulemaking bodies as “the simultaneous privatization and internationalization of governance” (Büthe & Mattli 2011, p. 5). This can be understood as an instance of transnational delegation. The authors view this shift as “privatization” because the rulemaking bodies in both sectors are international private sector bodies that are uncontested focal institutions. Unlike private certiﬁcation schemes, these are non-market private international standard-setting bodies, whose standards dominate the private sector. They include standard- setting bodies like the International Organization for Standardization and the International Electrotechnical Commission. Büthe and Mattli’s work provides several key insights into transnational delegation. First, they point out that “global private regulation has in recent years become vastly more important and is now a phenomenon of considerable social and economic consequence—it matters to understand it” (Büthe & Mattli 2011, p. 10). That is, they provide an over- view of global governance in the areas of global ﬁnance and global products and convincingly demonstrate both the breadth and the impacts of transnational delegation. For instance, they describe that in a few short decades after its cre- ation in 1947, the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) went from creating standards on basic mate- rials in engineering to regulating products as diverse as chemical technology, alternative energy, and air and water quality (Büthe & Mattli 2011, p. 132). Second, they describe the political implications of these standard-setting entities. They ask: Who wins and who loses when the rulemakers are private bodies? They ﬁnd that the answer depends not only on the ﬁnancial and epistemic resources of private actors, but also on domestic institutions. Hierarchical domestic institutions allow stakeholders to “speak with a single voice and in a timely fashion on the global stage” (Büthe & Mattli 2011, p. 13). These ﬁndings have broader implications when considered in context with the effects of economic globalization. As the authors point out, “[i]nternational trade law encourages the use of international standards…[and this] increasingly entail[s] referencing the pertinent standards of private transnational bodies” (Büthe & Mattli, 2011, p. 205). Thus, transnational delegation can have potentially far-reaching impacts. Although she is concerned with the practice of delegation more broadly (primarily to IOs), Koremenos’ work also provides insights about when and why states delegate to non-state actors. She ﬁnds that delegation is “widespread” and Delegation in environmental governance J. F. Green © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Page 4: is more likely when a large number of states are trying to solve complex cooperation problems (Koremenos 2008). Her study analyzes a random sample of 97 multilateral United Nations treaties in the areas of economics, environment, hu- man rights, and security. Koremenos examines both “internal delegation,” where states delegate to a subgroup within the agreement, and “external delegation,” where states delegate to a third party. When both types of delegation are in- cluded, delegation occurs in more than half of the treaties. External delegation occurs in roughly 40 percent of treaties.1 In cases of external delegation, she notes that six agreements mention NGOs – one type of non-state actor. Thus, in Koremenos’ work, delegation to non-state actors occurs in approximately 6 percent of the sample (i.e. six of the 97 agreements); the majority of delegation occurs in human rights agreements. In general, she ﬁnds that delegation can be explained by theories of “rational design” (Koremenos et al. 2001). That is, delegation is a functional response to a cooperation problem. Dispute resolution is the most frequently delegated task, while delegation to a third party solves the challenges of enforcement and commitment by enlisting an impartial third party. There are other case studies of transnational delegation that also inform this investigation. Green (2008) and Lund (2010) examine the challenges of delegating to private quality assurance ﬁrms in the Clean Development Mechanism of the Kyoto Protocol. They point to institutional design features that could potentially incentivize dishonest behavior. Given these potential conﬂicts of interest, their work raises questions about whether non-state actors are desirable agents in this instance. A large body of legal literature on global administrative law considers the “mechanisms, principles, practices, and supporting social understandings that promote or otherwise affect the accountability of global administrative bodies” (Kingsbury et al. 2005, p. 17). Frequently, these administrative bodies are private. Thus, casebooks on global admin- istrative law demonstrate that transnational delegation is present in many diverse corners of the global political land- scape. Private credit rating agencies, such as Moody’s and Standard & Poor’s determine the credit-worthiness of ﬁrms, which are, in turn, incorporated into public regulatory standards on banking. Transnational delegation is also a prominent feature of Internet regulation. The Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers (ICANN) is a private US corporation that has been delegated the authority to manage the address system of the Internet (al- though this will soon be transferred to an international body). Even doping in world sport is under the regulatory authority of non-state agents. The World Anti-Doping Agency is a Swiss NGO. It was delegated authority by the In- ternational Olympic Committee to set standards about prohibited substances and training methods, as well as pro- cedures for testing and disciplining infractions (Casini 2009, p. 433). The World Anti-Doping Agency’s rules and practices have become the basis for domestic regulations to implement the International Convention Against Doping in Sport. This brief snapshot of transnational delegation shows both the surprising breadth and depth of a phenomenon that is not generally recognized as an object of scholarly study. Non-state actors are performing transnational regulatory roles in a diverse set of issue areas, sometimes with great political effect. However, most research on delegation at the international level focuses on IOs. Because it has been spread across a variety of different literature, the little work that has been done on delegation to non-state actors lacks a theoretical home. One goal of this exploration is to identify this phenomenon and provide a systematic review of how it operates. The choice of the environmental arena as the focus of study also merits explanation. I consider this a “most-likely” case of delegation to private actors, because environment is seen as particularly permeable to non-state actors (Princen & Finger 1994). If delegation to non-state actors is a widespread phenomenon, then we should expect to see it in environmental politics. 4. The data The data presented moves beyond frameworks and case studies to provide a historical account of delegation practices in the area of environmental politics. In turn, this allows for descriptive inference, in which I make generalizations about delegation practices based on the observed variation. I created a new dataset that maps patterns of delegation in multilateral environmental agreements (MEAs). The dataset is built on Mitchell’s data on MEAs (Mitchell 2002–2016). Mitchell’s dataset is a random sample of 152 mul- tilateral environmental treaties from 1902 to 2002, drawn from all extant MEAs drafted between 1857 and 2002. In his data, each article of each agreement is coded for speciﬁc legal and institutional features. The agreements cover a range of environmental issues including energy, freshwater resources, habitat, oceans, pollution, and species.2 J. F. Green Delegation in environmental governance 4 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 5: My data builds on his random sample. I used a subset of Mitchell’s codes, including only those codes (and therefore treaty text) that contained information relevant to my inquiry. Speciﬁcally, I excluded 14 codes that were clearly irrelevant to the substance of the treaty, for example, codes that listed the title of the treaty, the list of countries that signed, and the URL that contained the text of the treaty. Any borderline codes, which could possibly include useful data, were included in my dataset. I am conﬁdent that this did not exclude any data containing information on dele- gation, and certainly not in any systematic way that would bias the ﬁndings. After omitting these codes from the sample, I developed the coding instrument, which was then pre-tested on a separate set of treaties. The selection process yielded 1,490 articles from across the 152 MEAs. I then read each article and coded it for two attributes: policy function and actor responsible. Each article could include multiple actors and multiple policy func- tions (these functions are described further). This process yielded 1,857 separate policy functions contained in the 152 treaties. The unit of analysis is the policy function, of which there are ﬁve: rulemaking, adjudication, implementa- tion, monitoring, and enforcement. These categories, and the associated coding instrument are based on the existing literature on delegation and the regulatory process.3 I recorded coding decisions using the Coding Analysis Toolkit (CAT), a web-based software program used for cod- ing qualitative data. The CAT provides a platform for users to code raw text. It does not engage in automatic coding through word searches or algorithms; rather it provides an interface for users to develop and pretest coding instru- ments, and then code, review, and annotate data.4 In sum, the difﬁcult work of conceptualizing the variables and coding the data remains the responsibility of the researcher.5 After developing the coding instrument, I checked the codes for consistency with the only other similar large-N dataset (to my knowledge), developed by Koremenos.6 Her instrument contains many more “tasks” (what I have termed functions) than mine, but are largely comparable in substance. While absolute comparability across qualitative datasets is not possible, this triangulation provides a useful indication that the logic of the coding and categories are robust to others’ speciﬁcations. Each policy function comprises distinct activities. Rulemaking includes the authority to make or amend treaties, as well as “to create administrative rules, to implement, ﬁll gaps in, or interpret preexisting international obligations” (Bradley & Kelley 2008, p. 12). Implementation is coded as instances in which actors “carry out agreed-upon programs and projects, including internal administrative tasks” (Bradley & Kelley 2008, p. 14). Conducting speciﬁc projects, training, capacity building, and technology transfer are all considered aspects of treaty implementation, as is general scientiﬁc research. All discussion of budget, ﬁnances, and ﬁnancial mechanisms is also coded as implementation. While implementation may seem to be simply the technical execution of pre-speciﬁed rules, it is not devoid of the exercise of authority. Actors charged with implementation must make decisions about how to achieve the goals that states have set. This process involves a variety of decisions about policy design. An example from the data demonstrates that mere acts of “implementation” also involve the exercise of authority. Article IV of the 1946 Whaling Convention states that the International Whaling Commission may collaborate with public or private agencies to: a encourage, recommend, or if necessary, organize studies and investigations relating to whales and whaling; b collect and analyze statistical information concerning the current condition and trend of the whale stocks and the effects of whaling activities thereon; c study, appraise, and disseminate information concerning methods of maintaining and increasing the populations of whale stocks. Clearly, decisions about who will carry out studies, and on what subjects can have profound effects on the policymaking process. Moreover, the outputs of these studies, which make judgments about the state of the species, can also affect states’ behavior. Thus, because authority is a type of power, which implies consent on the part of those subject to that power, even “lesser acts” of implementation can be considered a form of authority (Friedman 1990; Raz 1990; Lake 2009). Monitoring comprises any collection or dissemination of information. It can range from self-reporting by states to instances in which third parties inspect and verify compliance. Carrying out scientiﬁc assessments about speciﬁc topics related to rules (catch limits, deforestation levels, etc.) or updating information to reﬂect scientiﬁc knowledge are also coded as monitoring activities. Monitoring in the Convention on the Trade in Endangered Species, for example, occurs in three phases. First, states report to the Secretariat about trade in protected species (listed in the Annexes of the treaty) occurring within their borders. Then, the Secretariat communicates any problems to the relevant Management Delegation in environmental governance J. F. Green © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Page 6: Authorities established by the treaty (Article 13). Finally, the Management Authority must then report about the relevant laws and propose remedial actions as needed. Although conceptually, monitoring and adjudication are closely related, in this study, adjudication is limited to decisionmaking with respect to a dispute or controversy (Bradley & Kelley 2008, p. 12). Adjudication encompasses all authority to resolve disputes. In cases where adjudication includes provisions for corrective measures or sanctions, it is also coded as enforcement. Finally, enforcement includes activities that initiate or conduct investigations about compliance with the rules, intervention in other states’ behavior, imposing sanctions or retaliatory actions, or mandating that corrective actions be taken in response to non-compliance. For instance, the aforementioned Tropical Timber Agreement allows the governing body of the treaty to eject non-compliant members. Article 40 states, “If the Council decides that any member is in breach of its obligations under this Agreement and decides further that such breach signiﬁcantly impairs the operation of this Agreement, it may, by special vote, exclude that member from this Agreement” (see Mitchell 2002–2016). In some cases, an article within the treaty speciﬁes that more than one actor is responsible for a given policy func- tion. For instance, the 1935 Convention For The Campaign Against Contagious Diseases Of Animals requires that states report on domestic organizations qualiﬁed to implement the treaty – an implementation function that is not delegated. However, the same article also requires that the League of Nations disseminate these reports to all other parties – an act of delegation to an IO. Thus, multiple actors may be assigned to the same function– a reality of the complexities of international environmental lawmaking. Moreover, a given function may be simultaneously delegated and not delegated. For instance, in the Whaling ex- ample cited above, when organizing scientiﬁc investigations, the Commission may choose to do so “in collaboration with or through independent agencies of the Contracting Governments or other public or private agencies, establish- ments, or organizations, or independently.” Thus, the Commission may choose to delegate this implementation function, but need not do so; as a result, it is coded simultaneously as delegated and not delegated. The four possible actors responsible for these activities include: states themselves (i.e. no delegation), a subset of states, an IO, or a non-state actor. Delegation occurs when one of the latter three actors is responsible for a policy function. For example, in the Convention on Long-Range Transboundary Air Pollution, the treaty states that the Executive Secretary “shall carry out…secretariat functions” including convening meetings, transmitting information to the Parties, and executing other tasks outlined by the Executive Body. This is coded as an act of delegation to an IO (the Secretariat), responsible for implementation. Because governance does not end with the signing of a treaty, I also coded decisions made by parties after the treaty entered into force. “Post-treaty” decisions are the nuts and bolts of global environmental governance: they constitute administrative rulemaking about how to further the goals of the treaty. As noted above, many MEAs create a governing body called the Conference of the Parties, which meets regularly to review progress and make further rules. Post-treaty data is drawn from the decisions made at these regular meetings. I coded 518 post-treaty decisions for the same variables: policy function and actor responsible for that policy function. Post-treaty decisions help to provide a more complete picture of the practice of delegation. The crafting of a treaty has high political stakes (Koremenos et al., 2001). It is precisely for this reason that states shy away from harsh penalties for non-compliance or insert “escape clauses” (Rosendorff & Milner 2001). Generally speaking, post-treaty decisions are less highly legalized, because they do not require the same level of consensus to amend (Abbott et al., 2000). Treaties either specify a procedure for amendment, or require consensus among all signatories. Post-treaty decisions, by con- trast, are often made and approved by sub-bodies of the treaty; thus, the number of veto points is generally lower, as is the threshold for change. Examining delegation at the post-treaty level, therefore, provides additional information about state practice when decisions are more easily reversed, and the political stakes are generally lower. The post-treaty data is intended to provide a comparison with delegation practice at the treaty level. However, be- cause the data are not representative, I do not draw inferences from them. Only 44 of the 152 MEAs have post-treaty decisions available electronically. I drew a random sample of post-treaty decisions from among these 44 treaties, which run from 1946 until 2008. I used the same coding rules to determine whether or not delegation occurred, the agents selected, and the policy functions named. There were a number of challenges in sampling the post-treaty data. First, sub-treaty decisions vary considerably in length, ranging from one to hundreds of pages. In short, the problem here is that not all post-treaty decisions are equiv- alent: some contain only one decision, while others contain many. To address this problem, I stratiﬁed the sample into J. F. Green Delegation in environmental governance 6 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 7: two groups. In the ﬁrst group, there was one decision per document (n = 3198 decisions). I simply used a random number generator to select which documents to code. In the second, I used a random selection of decisions within documents (n = 280 documents).7 In addition to sampling challenges, post-treaty decisions are likely to suffer from some selection issues. First, post-treaty decisionmaking is not independent; it is a direct result of decisions made (or not made) in the treaty. Second, the majority of treaties with electronic post-treaty decisions are, unsurprisingly, more recent. The earliest treaty with electronically available post-treaty decisions is the 1949 Asia-Paciﬁc Fishery Commission. Recall that the treaty data begins in 1902; 9.2 percent of treaties in the sample are signed before 1949. It is likely that there is an upward bias in the post-treaty decisions, because those treaties that meet regularly are apt to be more complex and require more management. While this fact might inﬂate the overall rates of delegation, there is no reason to believe that any speciﬁc policy function is overrepresented, nor that there should be systematic differences between groups of agents. In other words, there is no reason to believe that upward bias should skew differences among groups of agents. Thus, the distribution of functions should be an accurate estimate of the sampling frame. The best solution in this situation is to acknowledge the potential sources and direction of bias (King et al. 1994). 5. Descriptive statistics 5.1. Treaty level Table 1 shows the distribution of policy functions across the 152 treaties, irrespective of the actor responsible. Because there are multiple policy functions per treaty, the total N is 1,847. Implementation occurs most frequently, followed by rulemaking. Adjudication and enforcement are the least frequently occurring. This is consistent with the oft-made ob- servation that international environmental law is relatively weak, because most legal agreements lack enforcement mechanisms (Najam 2005; Bodansky 2011). Table 2 shows the distribution of actors responsible for each policy function. Here, the ﬁrst important ﬁnding is that delegation – to any actor – is relatively rare. Less than a third of all policy functions are delegated to an agent – state, IO, or non-state actor. This ﬁnding stands in contrast to Koremenos’ work, which ﬁnds that delegation is “widespread,” occurring in more than half of the agreements in her sample (Koremenos 2008, p. 159). This seeming inconsistency can be reconciled by a closer examination of the unit of analysis. Koremenos analyzes patterns of delegation by treaty. Indeed, at the treaty level, I ﬁnd an even higher rate than Koremenos, with 80 percent of treaties including some form of delegation (to any type of agent, including a subgroup of states). More- over, in my dataset, roughly half of the treaties delegate half of the policy functions to some agent or another. That is, within a given treaty, there is considerable variation in rates of delegation. Interestingly, Koremenos also ﬁnds that treaties seldom delegate the same task to more than one agent (2008, pp. 162–3). By contrast, I ﬁnd that this is frequently the case. 25 percent of all policy functions delegated enlist more than one actor for the job. Moreover, while 3.8 percent of policy functions are delegated to non-state actors, they only serve as the sole agent in less than 1 percent of all policy functions. Therefore, in the vast majority of cases where states del- egate to non-state actors, they also choose another agent. Finally, another study by Koremenos (2007) ﬁnds that dispute resolution is very common; half of all treaties in her random sample across four issue areas contain dispute resolution provisions. She subdivides dispute resolution into dif- ferent categories, including adjudication, which she deﬁnes as instances “where a court steps in to make a ruling for the disputants” (Koremenos 2007, p. 198). In the subset of the sample that includes environmental agreements, she ﬁnds that adjudication occurs in half of the treaties.8 Across all four issue areas, she ﬁnds adjudication is delegated in 25 percent of the treaties studied. By contrast, at the treaty level, I ﬁnd that a much smaller proportion, only 9 percent, of all treaties delegate adjudication. However, at the level of the policy function, this number grows considerably: 61 per- cent of all adjudication policy functions are delegated. Table 2 also demonstrates that non-state actors play a small role as agents in environmental governance. They serve as agents in only 3.8 percent of all policy functions. This includes 20 percent of all of the treaties in the sample. By con- trast, 21.8 percent of all functions are delegated to IOs. Koremenos ﬁnds delegation to NGOs in only 6 percent of the agreements in her sample; however, her exclusive focus on NGOs – as opposed to other types of non-state actors – is much more restrictive than the operationalization in this study (2008, pp. 156, 159). This comparison demonstrates that the unit of analysis is critical in understanding delegation and the role of private actors as agents. At the level of the treaty, one might conclude that delegation is fairly common, as both my data and Koremenos’ indicate. However, their overall share of governing – as measured by policy function – is substantially lower according to my data. It appears that using the policy function as the unit of analysis provides a much more accurate picture of who is actually charged with governing. The analysis above shows that a better understanding of the role of private actors as agents requires a focus on the number and type of policy functions delegated. To lend further clarity to the precise nature of delegation, I examine instances in which they are the sole agents tasked with a given function. Recall that the coding scheme allows for more than one actor to be responsible for a policy function. Thus, states may choose to simultaneously delegate to an agent and be responsible for it themselves. For ex- ample, a treaty may require that states self-report their ﬁsh catches (coded as monitoring), but then also mandate that the Secretariat disseminate that information to other parties (also coded as monitoring). In that instance, monitoring is both delegated and not delegated. Table 3 shows the instances in which private actors serve as sole agents, by policy function. Out of the 1,847 policy functions in the dataset, 70 are delegated to non-state agents, and of these 70, only 19 are delegated solely to non-state agents – less than 1 percent. The majority of the time they govern in tandem with other actors. This ﬁnding can be interpreted in two ways. First, it could support the view that non-state actors are rarely seen as having a unique com- parative advantage in governing. Alternatively, it may demonstrate more simply that governments prefer not to have non-state actors in positions of authority.
